speaker: interviewer
text: April 19, 1996, Auschwitz. So please tell us where we are now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now in the infamous Block 11. That anybody that was a longer time in Auschwitz knew was called the torture bunker. Anybody that was involved-- according to German or Nazi understanding was involved in any conspiracy whatsoever, or was considered a potential escapee, so to say, of one type, was brought here, was tortured here, was interrogated here. And if they-- eventually the torturer and the investigator came to a conclusion that this man is a danger to the security of the SS, was then shot here at the shooting wall. There is a material-- can you see from here in the back-- like caulk that absorbs noise, because all around people-- prisoners were in the barracks, and they didn't want to frighten them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to absorb the shootings. My brother and two of his friends got involved in the underground. And in-- in September of 1944, almost three years later-- two and a half years later-- were arrested one night and were brought here and tortured.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that because the underground had a good communication, and within 24 hours we knew-- not only me, people that were involved in the underground knew that the three boys were brought here, were tortured, and eventually they came back, and I will talk about it later when the-- they were hanged in front of 15,000 people. The truth is that they were so strong that although there were other people involved in the conspiracy, nobody was in addition arrested. So I have to say they were strong enough to withstand all the torture and not to betray the co-conspirators.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what were the ways of torturing them that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were hanging them on-- on the hands, hanging them on the feet, beating them. Even electric shocks. All the-- the-- the usual torture methods, they were known. And maybe they invented a few more because the SS was very good at that. But they were tortured severely. That, we know. Because most people really died during the torture and during the investigation. They were probably saved only because they had in mind a diabolic [GERMAN], a-- a-- a kind of a show to frighten others not to try the same thing that they did. Otherwise probably they-- they would have never been brought back. There's-- there is logic to this. Because as I said, later-- two weeks later, they were brought back to-- to the Buna camp, to the Auschwitz III camp, and were hanged in front of 15,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did informations about what was going on on that block were going out of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I was a minor member of the underground. My brother and his two friends were major members of the underground. They were deeply involved. And there was an underground in Auschwitz too. As a matter of fact, most people-- must have been-- the Polish people must have been familiar. The president of Poland, Cyrankiewicz, was in Auschwitz three years. And he was the head of the underground. On the Jewish side, there was a name [? Leiser ?] [NON-ENGLISH]. He's still alive. He's still involved. So the guy is over 80 years old. He was the deputy head of the underground. They both, of course, were communists. And they had a communication system, because constantly SS convoys went from Auschwitz to Buna, from Buna to Auschwitz, from Auschwitz to Birkenau, every single day. And it was not very difficult to transmit information. That was very easy, relatively.

speaker: interviewer
text: So shall we go up the ways?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then-- and then we'll go--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Birkenau. OK. [INAUDIBLE]. So please, please tell us where we are now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And here we are right next to the high voltage double fence where so many people ended their life. It was so close and so easy that in their despair, people that didn't have any more the strength, the will, to survive, it was an easy death. They ran here and either from those turrets, you see here, there were in most instance two or three SS men day and night, and there were no windows at the time. At least they didn't show. It was open. They were standing with heavy machine guns. And the minute anybody even dared to get as close as five yards to the wire, they were shooting already. And still, so many times they were-- how you call this-- like fleas at the-- at the-- where-- there are flea thing. Strips. And fleas get it. Thus they were-- how do you say-- wait a minute-- let me think. Like a-- like a stamp. They were-- were-- were tied to it, why-- why-- why-- almost like by-- by-- by glue. They were glued to the wire after they ran here. And interesting enough, the people that despaired the fastest were intellectuals, not simple people. Simple people were to a degree in Poland used to hardship. But intellectuals, they were really deeply thinking what is happening here. They just could not accept the fact that in the-- in the-- in the century of enlightenment, that human beings can be so inhuman, can do this in an industrialized way. Here was killing going on like an industry, like a killing industry. That this was passable-- possible in the 20th century. I myself was so thirsty for knowledge because they took it out of school. And I many times had discussions with those professors, doctors, architects, ministers, high officials of government, and-- and-- and they were so in despair. They just could not accept that this-- a world where this is possible. And many of those people committed suicide by running against the-- the barbed wire. And then they were glued to it like flies. Literally. They were dead. That's it. That-- myself, in all honesty, I never-- never in all the years that I was in concentration camp-- had any desire to die like this. In spite of everything that was going on. You know, sometimes, in-- in looking back, I remember I literally created an invisible wall and told myself what's happening here, that is all a nightmare. It's not really happening. Because otherwise, you lose your sanity. And on top of this, I swore to myself, those bastards, I'm going to outlive them. Literally. The more brutal they became, the more I made up my mind, I am going to outlive you. And I-- I used all my-- all the finesse, all the shrewdness, and almost an-- I developed an instinctive ability, a sixth sense, to evade danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the danger was lurking every minute, every hour. You could go right out of the barrack and a drunk SS man came by, didn't like your face, didn't like how you saluted him, you know? Anywhere in the concentration camp, whenever you saw an SS man, had to stop, and pull direct from the head, and-- and-- and literally say-- not say your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] number 1969925. You had to-- to declare your number on attention. That was the way how you had to treat an SS man. And if he didn't like you or he was too drunk, he just beat you to death, or beat you so badly that it was a question of days, and you were a candidate for the gas chambers. That was the life in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Have you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it-- it-- it-- it is hard to-- to explain it. It was so terrible that you never knew if you live the next day. The only, call it luxury, we had is when we were finally released and we could sleep. And we learned to sleep under any conditions. Under any conditions. If it was on straw, if it was on-- on wood, whatever. Because that was the only way that you could cut out this horrible life and-- and-- and be-- and dream of a world that doesn't exist anymore. Otherwise, the next day you didn't have the strength to survive another day. Sleeping was the only luxury that we thought about it. And-- and interestingly enough, we were dreaming about food. It was like an obsession. We were dreaming as if we are free. And my parents-- our parents took us to a restaurant. We were eating. It was an obsession with food, because we were starving all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever thought about escaping from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- let me say something interesting about escaping. Any time somebody escaped, in-- in-- in [GERMAN]. How do you say that? In-- to punish us, they in most instances killed 100, sometimes 200 people, at random. They went when we were on roll call. Every third one. Out, out, out. And killed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, any human being with a conscience and-- would feel how-- what right do I have to save my life and to condemn 100 innocent people, sometimes my uncles, my relatives, my cousin, my friends, to get killed for me? There was an unwritten code in the underground. Nobody escapes without permission from the underground, because there is no morality in this.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you save yourself, and 100 have to pay for you. Because out of the 100, there were some they had a chance to survive. So we really would condemn him to die. That was an underwritten law. On the other hand, at the-- closer to the end of the war, a mass escape-- that is a different story. Because we knew that the SS had the master plan to kill, to annihilate, all the inmates of the camps so that the-- the Russians and the American would never know what was happening here. That-- that-- we knew that this plan exists. That's a different story.

speaker: interviewee
text: But individual on their own-- and still, individuals did escape. Were selfish and did escape. And in most instances were caught, brought back, and then they had to go around with-- with-- with a [GERMAN]. How you saw this? With-- with a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say in English. And then, I escaped. I am back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Drum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? With a drum. They had to-- to hit a drum, and went around the whole camp. And were-- were-- had to proclaim, I escaped and I am back. I am back. And then they were hanged. OK? So the chances if you did only escape to stay free was very, very minimal. Standing here at the entrance of the former concentration camp Auschwitz Buna Monowitz. For me, it's very sad to see that there is absolutely nothing left of the camp except this monument. And the plaque that we have here in front of us reads as follows. 'Here in the years of 1941 to 1945 upon the site of the former extermination camp, Auschwitz Buna Monowitz, the Nazi perpetrators of the genocide killed about 30,000 political and war prisoners of various nations. We honor their memory. The community of Oswiecim district.' This is the plaque. I am sad again because I know that 94-- 95%-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --of the prisoners were Jews. Since this monument was established under communist rule, they didn't even bother to state this important fact. This is the grave site of 9-- of-- not of 30,000, but I know for sure of at least 55,000 Jews that-- who really came from all over Europe. From Germany, from Poland, from the Ukraine, from Greece, from Norway. All worked here and were at most killed over the years. Myself, I spent the best part of my youth from 1942 to 1945 in this camp. And I look back on the events that took part here. I'm getting very sad and very angry, because I feel they had no right to destroy this camp. And they had no right to eliminate the fact that 95% of the people that were killed there were Jewish people. For me, I have very sad memories because this is the place where I was together with my brother until October 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when my brother and two of his friends were hanged in front of 15,000 prisoners who were forced to stand there and to watch the show that the Nazis put up. Let me tell you a little bit why my brother and his two friends were hanged. They were active members of the underground. In their life, in their death, they were really an example how human beings should behave in the inferno of Auschwitz under the worst circumstances. They showed so much character, goodwill, and commitment, that anybody that was in Buna will never forget in this life what was going on on this fateful day on October the 9th, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The underground was very active in the Buna camp. And I dare to say that thousands-- literally thousands of prisoners-- were saved from certain death because the underground was there. They manipulated the SS. They did hide people that were supposed to go on selections. Almost every two weeks, an SS doctor came here with some SS orderlies and made selections. First, they did it in the sick barracks. They called it in German [GERMAN], where prisoners were kept until the next transport to the gas chambers. Medicine was very rare in supply. Very seldom was somebody real, seriously sick, and-- and was healed in-- in the [GERMAN]. Only people that had superficial sickness did survive because they got a bit more food. And there were a lot of Jewish medicine-- orderlies. Jewish prisoners that-- thank God-- and there were a very few Jewish doctors. Thank God that they were there. They had more sensitivity, and they took care of the prisoners. There was also a Lageralteste of the [GERMAN] who was a Polish prisoner who did not behave very well. I-- we had all the oppression that this man was anti-Semitic. But that's a story for itself. Let me tell you a story, and it probably was one of the most dramatic stories in the whole gray history of Auschwitz. Those three prisoners, Leo Diament, Janek Grossfeld, and Nathan Weissmann were [GERMAN]. That means camp builder, so to speak. They corrected the buildings in the SS barracks. They corrected the builds-- certain small buildings in the camp. And because they had to build, they also got shipments of material, and had certain contacts with civilians from the outside that brought the material in. And they used every connection that they had to save people's life. Every night in a small washroom, dozens, sometimes 50 people came, and received a little soup. They went, for example, to the kitchen of the SS and all the leftovers were mostly thrown away. They went there and some of them manipulated under disguise of bringing building materials into-- into the camp, brought food in the camp. And the truth is that the difference of life and death in Buna was half a little soup more. Even it was dish water soup, made a difference if a man will die or a man will survive. Also, I must state here clear and-- very clearly. One of the biggest problems in the camps was that the SS tried systematically to dehumanize us. In other words, to reduce us to the level of animals. And in some instance, they were successful. But by and large, because of people-- of Janek and Leo and Nathan-- they did not succeed, because through their conduct they showed character strengths, moral strengths, and-- and and were-- they literally served as a role model.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people looking at them, how they behaved, how they devoted their life to save others, said to themselves, why should we become animals? Why can't we also have the strength to behave humanly? So let's put it this way. To stay human, to preserve your humanity in the inferno of Auschwitz, was a great act of courage. And at same time, an act of resistance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the underground knew of a plan by the SS in case of a-- an attack by the Russian forces, who were already about 25, 30 miles away from Auschwitz, to destroy the camp totally and to kill everybody so that there would be no traces left that ever there was a camp. How did they want to accomplish this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a diabolic plan. They wanted to use heavy artillery that the Germans conquered from the Russians. I'm talking about Russian artillery and Russian ammunition. And from a certain distance, they wanted literally to shoot the whole camp with its inhabitants to rubbles. And then have the chutzpah, the audacity, to claim the Russians did it. Not they did it. That was the plan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you heard about this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the underground. The underground has such connection, we-- there was a British prisoner of war camp. They even had a transmitter, that they transmitted to-- to BBC London. Every day, another transport with 4,000 people came from Sarajevo. This came from-- from-- from Norway. This came from-- from-- from no. From Greece. From Bulgaria. From anywhere in Europe, they came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it was an unwritten law by the underground that nobody escapes. Why? If one man escaped, at least they killed 100 to show-- to-- to-- to literally-- to terrorize the people not to do it. And after all, nobody had a right to save himself and then cause the deaths of 100 innocent other people. So therefore, very few people that were connected with underground did escape. Others on their own did escape. And indeed, we had to stay sometimes 24 hours without food in cold winter, without a-- a-- a toilet. Just standing and-- and waiting until they found him.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they found him, then they had to run around with a-- with a-- a-- a-- a-- a drum, and-- and hitting the drum all over, and screaming, I am back. And then they were hanged in front of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those three young men-- Leo, Nathan, and Janek-- volunteered to break out from the camp and to contact-- to get in contact with partisans. Partisans, whoever doesn't know that, are guerrilla fighters that were already very active around Auschwitz in the last months of the year 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard shooting and explosives every night, practically. Now, their plan was to organize partisans and in connection with the electrician in the camp, to cause a short in the high voltage of the double voltage fence, and then organize a mass outbreak. Because if the Nazis will kill everybody, if we succeed to save 20%, 30% of the population, we have accomplished a lot. Unfortunately, in the last minute, their plan was betrayed by a Polish electrician who was part of the conspiracy. Now, what he did is they took the three in the middle of the night to Auschwitz, to the main camp, and put them into the Block 11, which is called the torture bunker. And they were there between three and four weeks. And we know from reports that the underground-- again, got from the underground in Auschwitz I-- that they were tortured and tortured and tortured. And the truth is that no information was given because there were at least 10 more people connected to that conspiracy, and nobody was arrested. Only the three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the question is asked, why did they kill them? That we found out later. One night, we came home from work. The exact date is October the 9th. And while the columns went through the gate, of course there was the orchestra, and playing those snappy SS march, everybody had to-- to march. And instead of going to their barracks-- because as I said before, the only pleasure we had was sleeping. And the faster we got home into barracks, the better it was for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They march them onto parade ground where roll call was taken. And everything was dark. And suddenly, they built reflectors and to the total surprise, you saw three big gallows with a big platform. They couldn't believe it, what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was saying, who is going to be the victim? Nobody knew what was going on, including myself. I had no idea. Suddenly, three SS companies all armed with sub-machine guns and five heavy machine guns came in, surrounded in a half circle the whole parade ground where the prisoners were standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Lageralteste-- that mean the camp elder, an old professional criminal, that's the way they called him-- went-- there was a little bunker in a restroom in the camp, and they brought the three. They came with their hand tied in the back, their head high up, proud. They-- they mounted the gallows. And we have seen in Buna many hangings. And in most instances, the people were crying that they have to die. Here, we have three young men, instead of crying they were so-- I mean, in French they call this [FRENCH]. They had the spiritual strength not only not to cry. But while they were standing on the crates, and the SS put the noose around their head, they raised their hand and shouted that everybody could hear it. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Keep your head high. Have courage. Because we are the last ones. They had the-- the strength not only not to cry. They were consoling us, we should not despair. We should have courage to stick to it, to have a will to survive. And then they put the noose over at the first two. And while they were hanging there, they went and-- and pulled their feet so to speed up the death. And the last one was my brother, Leo. His last words also. He raised his hands and shouted with all the strength of his voice, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Long live liberty. Then they put the noose about him, and he was also hanged. And then everybody-- all the 15,000 prisoners-- had to file by in single lines to see what was happening there. And they exactly, exactly, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] accomplished the opposite. They wanted to terrorize us. They wanted to despair us. And here, by their heroic death, did exact opposite. They were all went dry, and everybody was proud. Proud as Jews. Proud as human beings. And you know, people were so shocked. And they were so touched that they just couldn't cry. I couldn't even cry. Nobody could cry. But-- but that was an event that I myself will never forget in my life. My friend Sigi was there, and-- and-- and I have read this-- I wrote a little article about this. And every time we see-- we talk about it, we can see before our eyes how proud and heroic those three young Jewish men died in Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only say one thing. I had such-- how to say this? I had feelings of guilt. I couldn't do anything. I wish I could have done something to save their lives. In a way, I was fortunate that the SS did not know that I am in the camp. Because that they know, they probably will hang me too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a friend that worked in the political department that was an equivalent to the Gestapo in the camp. And there was my file. But my friend literally took the file out and was hiding it. Otherwise, I wouldn't be standing here and be able to tell you a story. Now, I want to say a few more words about the Buna camp. I think the Buna camp was the only camp where there were a lot of Jewish [GERMAN] and Jewish kapos. And I would say-- with a straight face I can say most of those people really helped save human lives. And the way it is, if one person does it, they inspired each other. In such a way that very few camps had this luck, I would say, that Jewish prisoners became [GERMAN] and kapos. Thank God for that. And I would say also on the death march from Auschwitz, when Auschwitz was evacuated, a lot of our friends were killed. But many did survive, more than from other camp, strictly because we had good people there, good Jews, that made it their task to organize whatever they could in soup, food, bread, and to give it to people that didn't have anything. To divide it among people so that they can survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What else can I say about Buna? Yeah. Something interesting also happened in the Buna camp. One day, we received a group of 40 young Jews that were on [HEBREW], that were on a farm to prepare themself to go to Palestine. And they were caught by the Nazis in and were brought back on block.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stuck together, an example of brotherly love, of brotherly friendship, of-- of human behavior, which was so unusual. We saved each other, not only by helping each other, by encouraging the other. And I will never forget another thing in the barrack. One night, I think it was in 1943, we even had a Hanukkah. We got together and celebrate Hanukkah. That was an event that gave us so much courage that I will never forget in my life. Of course, on January 18 they took us all out. And 75,000 prisoners of the Auschwitz complex were marched to Gleiwitz. They were loaded on cattle trains into Germany, away from the Russians. Unfortunately, tens of thousands died on this particular death march. I myself also was almost dead. It-- it's just a miracle that one night, we-- we reached a-- a-- a little town with the name Waldenburg. And a Russian artillery attack had a direct hit on the central generator, electricity generator station. And for almost two full minutes, it was all dark. And I just ran. And that was my luck. Why I took the courage, I was only totally-- totally emaciated. If I would have stayed with this march another three, four days, there is no doubt in my mind I would have given up and I wouldn't be alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ran all night like an animal. I mixed about German, ethnic German refugees, that were-- the-- the streets were full of them. They were running away from the Russians. I was an advance. I spoke German because I was born in Germany. And mixing a-- with them, that really saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I didn't know is that every kilometer was a checkpoint. And at each checkpoint, there were SS men that pulled out from the refugees any man from the age of 15 to 55, that he could force into the-- they called this [GERMAN], into a German army. And I was ordered twice to present myself to be recruited-- can you imagine-- into a German army. 10 minutes for the end of the war, so to speak. But-- yeah, I didn't know what to do first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had an idea. I was lucky that at that time, I spoke almost fluently French. And I pretended I am a forced laborer from France. I'm a Frenchman. They asked me for papers. I had a very good excuse. I told them that I was sick-- which is true. I had-- how do you say [GERMAN] in-- in-- in-- in English? I had-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: --a disease that you-- is in the blood, that you can prove. And I said, I was in the hospital. The Germans bombarded the hospital. I jumped from the hospital naked and merciful German gave me something to wear. Thank God I made the story up, and here I am, and have the honor today to honor the [? kiddushim, ?] which means the martyrs of Buna. And I maintain again that 95% of them were Jewish. And it wasn't 30,000. There were at least 55,000 Jews that were worked to death, beaten to death, and died in Buna, and were sent to gas chambers. So that's more or less what I have to say about it.
